evidence_quotes.quote: Gambling mechanics like loot boxes often hide the actual odds of receiving specific items, encouraging repeated spending with no clear expectation.
The EU's efforts to curb dark patterns, manipulative monetization, and unfair commercial practices are much needed.
Publishers can shut down multiplayer servers at any time, leaving users with no access to content they paid for.

paragraphs.text: As a digital consumer in Germany, I want to raise several serious concerns that directly relate to fairness, transparency, and equality in the online market, especially around digital platforms and the gaming industry. Valve's Steam does not allow German users to access certain adult-rated content that is available in other EU countries, even when that content is not pornographic or extreme. After recent changes to youth protection laws in Germany, many indie developers have had their games delisted because they cannot afford the costly classification process, making them unavailable locally. The real issue is that Steam offers no method for German users to verify their age, such as submitting ID, to access this content. This means that EU citizens are not being treated equally. I am willing to verify my age, and am set up for eID, but the platform simply does not provide the option. As a result, German users are locked out of legal content without cause, creating digital inequality within the EU. Either content should be available to all EU citizens or banned for all. Fair is fair. Another concern is the increasingly predatory nature of microtransactions in games. They are often no longer "micro" at all and can cost more than the base game. Shops rotate items using time-limited offers and fear-of-missing-out tactics, pressuring users to make impulsive decisions. The use of in-game currencies or "play money" hides the real-world cost of purchases, reducing transparency. Gambling mechanics like loot boxes often hide the actual odds of receiving specific items, encouraging repeated spending with no clear expectation. These practices are designed to drive maximum engagement and monetization through psychological pressure and addictive loops. This goes far beyond fair commercial practice and is especially harmful to vulnerable users. There is also the issue of digital ownership and post-purchase rights. Publishers can shut down multiplayer servers at any time, leaving users with no access to content they paid for. In many cases, users have made significant in-game purchases and are then left with nothing when the service ends. Unlike physical goods, these are digital products that could remain available if publishers chose to allow it. There is often no refund, no compensation, and no technical option for communities to take over support or hosting. Japan has laws requiring refunds when digital services are discontinued. The EU should consider similar consumer protections, or at minimum, require that publishers make the server code or tools available to the community once official support ends. As consumers, we expect fairness, transparency, and a consistent digital market across the EU. We should be able to access legal content regardless of national borders, see real prices before buying, and trust that the products we pay for will not disappear without warning or compensation. The EUs efforts to curb dark patterns, manipulative monetization, and unfair commercial practices are much needed. Please make sure they are applied rigorously in the digital entertainment and gaming sectors, where these problems are especially widespread.
